review:
Was mir gefällt ist der große Umfang an Fraktionen, die Schlachten und die Grafik. Was mir nicht gefällt ist die KI, wenn sie ihre ganze Armee in einer Burg oder Stadt versammelt und sie nichts erobert. 
Runden lang bewegt sie sich nur um die Stadt oder Burg. Die Gegner können dann einfach ihre Städte und Burgen erobern.
Was mir besonders gefallen würde, wäre wenn alle Fraktionen nach ihren Siegbedingungen spielen würden. Oder noch besser wäre es, wenn alle Fraktionen nach der Welteroberung streben würden. Selbst die Rebellen sollten diese Chance bekommen.
Ansonsten gute Arbeit, das ihr so ein Spiel herausgebracht habt.

What I don't like is when the AI ​​gathers its entire army in a castle or city and it doesn't conquer anything. For rounds it only moves around the city or castle. The opponents can then simply conquer their cities and castles. What I would particularly like is if all factions played according to their victory conditions. Or even better, it would be if all factions strived for world conquest. Even the rebels should have this chance. Otherwise, good job on releasing a game like this.

review:
Absolutely ridiculous cash grab game. The price alone is steep. They go from $60 to $0 and back to $60 within a week. The DLC is absurd. $421.16 of DLC? Nah.

Plus it employs (assuming it's still there) Denuvo Anti-Tamper. This, as I understand it, requires that your computer reach out and essentially get permission from their server to let you play the game you bought, downloaded, installed, and have been playing. If that server is unavailable or shut down (like if they want you to buy other games they release), your potentially $480+ of content becomes worthless.

There's a reason the Stop Killing Games movement exists. Unless the company promises to ensure the game does not require a connection, and can be played in perpetuity after purchase, I can't recommend this game to anyone. I only installed it and ran it for a minute or so to see if I actually had access to it during a free week/weekend event.

review:
It's had some rough moments over the years, but it's easy to say Total War Warhammer 3 has developed into a well-balanced and enjoyable strategy game.  Each faction has a different playstyle and the player can focus on growing power in a number of ways (diplomatically, militarily, subterfuge).  Not so much a fan of the evil factions (except Vampires), but enjoy the "good" factions.  

If you like melee centered factions, the Lizardmen might be your best bet, gunpowder - debatable between Empire and Dwarves (Chaos Dwarves artillery is terrifying, Dwarf Gunships are breathtaking).  Archers - High Elves and Wood Elves get some very strong ranged units.  Bretonnia's got Blessed Trebuchets that fire projectiles which only harm enemies - if you can force the enemy to blob up, this is a wicked combo.  Magic - debatable between High Elves (Teclis) and Empire (Gelt).  Trade - Eastern Kingdoms or Chaos Dwarves.

You can easily spend a few hundred hours playing various factions and easily a thousand or so hours if you play evil/good factions alike.

There's just two problems I have with the game presently:
Kislev: You gain +10 Ice Court support per attack against Chaos, and if you enjoy Crusading against Chaos as much as any human player ought to, you will start to attack lots of chaos enemies (because you'll be surrounded by them *all campaign*).  This means you'll start almost certainly out-gaining Patriarchy support, and if it becomes too unbalanced, you hit some fairly nasty faction penalties.  You can balance this out through careful building planning overtime, but in my case, I noticed the imbalance too late after attacking Chaos anywhere from 1 to as many as 6 times a turn over a long period of time - not a game breaker as far as problems, but definitely like a bad headache.

Dwarf Grudge System: A player can tend to grow very powerful late-game as Dwarves and eventually their grudge system becomes a bit unrealistic.  It's almost as if they'll run out of enemies eventually, but need enemies to hate, where you'll almost be tempted to keep minor factions alive just to generate and solve grudges.  I suppose it's somewhat realistic for the setting, with potential conflicts with "good" factions etc (like Elves), but just really can ruin a dwarf campaign in my opinion.

review:
This game has nice aesthetics, such as sound design, art style, character design and voice acting.

However the gameplay balance is horrendous for single player campaigns. In this game you have 2 options of style to play, auto resolve and field combat.

Field combat allows you to control and micromanage wars in a detailed map setting when you engage in combat.

Auto resolve allows you to combat the A.I without field combat based on the strength of your units.

I enjoy the auto resolve option seeing as though I don't get a lot of time through out my day to get into the field combat. This option is where the problem begins. When starting campaigns, your race are starting from scratch and you are put in a set scenario where your enemy is already strong and war ready.

By the grace of luck, your enemy may become your ally or target you and stop your campaign in less than 10 -15 turns. 

Be too pacifist, and you will get taken advantage of. Be too aggressive, and all the A.I enemies will dog pile on your settlements, all the while the other races were way ready for wars than you were. 

I like the idea of Warhammer RTS, but I can't recommend this game with the horrendous balance it has within it's campaign mode. Hopefully they will balance the mode where all races start from square one and not put players in scenarios with little to no resources to start out with.

The battles are fantastic, and they're the real pull, but every other mechanics is half-baked at best, and rather than improving the rest of the core systems they just keep making new lords with new minor mechanics unique only to them  which are all similarly lacklustre and dissapointing. You can make it so you almost always win auto-resolves, but you will run out of fun to have quite quickly even if you mod it heavily.

review:
[h2]The Feel[/h2]

I've been playing Total War games since the beginning — (first Shogun game). Since then I've played hundreds of hours across other titles in the franchise as well. I am not a pro, but I'd like to think I have a good understanding of how each game works and why they were good game experiences or not (looking at you, Thrones of Britannia…) Annoyingly, when I started with Warhammer Total War, all of that knowledge and game experience seems to be worth nothing.

Most of the time the game doesn't feel like a strategy game, but more like a slot machine. The game's flow is so incidental that I rarely feel it's me who's playing. Even if I manage to defeat an enemy army or occupy a settlement, it doesn't necessarily happen because I was clever or had good intuition about a military situation. It's like reading a badly written book where you cannot figure out who the murderer is, because the author introduces new elements you had no way of knowing before. You are not part of the story, you cannot immerse yourself in it — you're just watching things happen with or without you.

Even on easy difficulty and without the AI threat assessment, the game is so challenging that one cannot really get to know it or get experienced with at least one faction.

[h2]Game Mechanics[/h2]

I think this contradicts the game's complexity. It doesn't really matter how and why each race is so different. Even within one race the different factions can vary enormously, but it doesn't really add to the game. Also, unlike previous titles, nowhere does it indicate how difficult each faction is — in terms of starting location or military style. You constantly have to ask some AI what's going on, how each mechanic works, and what it means in gameplay terms.

[h2]Money Grab[/h2]

Considering the business side of the trilogy — how you need to own all three base games, plus the amount of DLCs you need to buy just to try out different races — is outrageous.

At this point I wish I could return the whole trilogy with all its DLCs. There are much better, more balanced games in the franchise.

(I had no performance issues, the game runs well on my specs.)

review:
Total War Warhammer 3 is genuinely one of the best games you can pick up if you love creating cinematic battles or if you love fantasy warfare with magic and steel. The scale, the unit variety, the particle effects, the lighting, it all comes together in a way that makes every clash feel massive and dramatic. As someone who enjoys crafting visuals and cinematic scenes, this game delivers a ton of raw material to work with which is why I love creating cinematic battles for this game along with others from the series.

I do wish Creative Assembly had added more kill‑sync animations or more detailed combat interactions. The game has the foundation for it, but those extra touches would have pushed the visuals even further. Thankfully, the modding community fills a lot of those gaps. With the right mods, your cinematic experience becomes richer, smoother, and way more immersive.

In terms of gameplay, Warhammer 3 is extremely accessible. Battles are easy to control, the campaign is straightforward to learn, and the factions all feel unique without being overwhelming. It’s a great entry in the Total War series and one I definitely recommend, especially if you’re into cinematic content creation like I am.

My only real complaint is the DLC pricing. It feels unfair and honestly a bit greedy at times. The content is good, but the cost adds up quickly, and it’s the one part of the experience that consistently leaves a bad taste.

Overall though, the game itself is excellent. The visuals, the gameplay, the campaign, the mod support, all of it makes Warhammer 3 a standout title in the Total War franchise.

review:
本体とすべてのDLCを購入しても、動画の黒十字を着けた王子やそれと口論しているドワーフの指導者では遊べない。別途、前々作である『Total War: Warhammer 1』の購入が必要。同様に、前作である『2』を購入しないと同席しているハイエルフの指導者も遊べない。そういったことが多く、全部購入すると定価は2万9000円を超える。料金体系が著しくわかりにくかったので、低評価とした。

しかしながら、ゲームそのものはとてもよい。『Total War』(総力戦)というタイトルに似つかわしく、大規模な戦闘をこれほど緻密かつ遊びやすく作られたRTSはそれほど多くない。特にゲーム性として、ユニットが完全に破壊されずに撤退できる場面が多く、破壊されても再雇用が容易なため、他のRTSや4Xでよくある『長い内政の末にたった1回の決戦で負けて滅亡する』というシナリオが発生しにくく、とてもよい。また、他のRTSとは異なり、隊列を維持しながら戦うことが基本のため、独特な操作方法が採用されており、例えば、現実の『テルシオ』などの隊形で戦うことも可能で、実演しているYoutuberさんなども多く、このあたりはとても面白い。

一方で、文明ごとの特色は『Age of Wonders: Planetfall』や『Starcraft 2』に比べると控えめで、個人的にはもう一声ほしい。あと、いわゆる『OP』(Over Powered、強すぎる)なユニットが多く、真面目に攻略するとそればかり生産することになり、単調になりがち。例えば、チュートリアル文明である『Kislev』(キスレフ)の『Kossars(Spears)』(コサール(槍))は優れた弓兵であるにもかかわらず、優れた槍兵でもあり、コストも普通の弓兵と変わらないため、ハードカウンターがロケット砲くらいしか存在しない。ロケット砲を出せない文明と戦っている場合、最上位のユニットを出せるようになるまで、コサール槍以外を生産するメリットは多分ない。

あと、ゲーム本体のファイルサイズが50GBを超える巨大なもののため、使っているパソコンやネット回線によっては、ダウンロードや遊ぶのに困難を感じるかもしれない。私は戦闘開始前のロード時間が少し長いなと感じるものの、戦闘中に重くなることはないので、個人的には許容範囲内。

review_english: Even if you purchase the main unit and all the DLC, you won't be able to play the prince wearing the black cross in the video or the dwarf leader who is arguing with him.You will need to separately purchase the previous work, Total War Warhammer 1.Similarly, unless you purchase the previous work, 2, you will not be able to play the high elf leader who is also present.If you purchase all of them, the list price will be over 29,000 yen.I gave it a low rating because the price system was extremely difficult to understand. However, the game itself is very good.As befits the title Total War, there aren't many RTS games that have made large-scale battles as precise and easy to play as this one.Especially in terms of gameplay, there are many scenes where units can retreat without being completely destroyed, and even if they are destroyed, they can be easily rehired, so the scenario where a unit loses and perishes in just one decisive battle after a long civil war, which is common in other RTSs and 4Xs, is less likely to occur.It's very good, and unlike other RTSs. Because the basic idea is to fight while maintaining formation, a unique control method is adopted.For example, it is also possible to fight in formations such as the real Tershio, and there are many YouTubers who demonstrate it.It is very interesting.On the other hand, the characteristics of each civilization are modest compared to Age of Wonders Planetfall and Starcraft 2.Personally, I would like to see more units.Also, there are many so-called OP (Over Powered) units. If you take it seriously, you will end up producing that much, which tends to become monotonous. For example, although the tutorial civilization Kislev's Kossars (Spears) are excellent archers, they are also excellent spearmen and cost the same as ordinary archers, so the only hard counter you have is a rocket cannon. If you are fighting a civilization that cannot produce rocket cannons, until you can produce the highest unit. There is probably no benefit to producing anything other than the Kosar spear.Also, the file size of the game itself is huge, over 50GB, so depending on the computer and internet connection you are using, you may have difficulty downloading and playing it.I feel that the loading time before the battle starts is a little long, but it doesn't get heavy during the battle, so it's within an acceptable range for me personally.

review:
This game is "fine"; certainly worth buying on sale, but not for full price. As far as ranking it within the TW franchise, the entire Warhammer series is a bit underwhelming. So much power has been directed into generals with RPG-style gear and abilities that the actual battle simulation loses a lot of its impact/enjoyment. I have fought battles in which my entire army is destroyed, but my broken vampire god character manages to single-handedly vanquish hundreds of troops to win the battle for me.

The battles have a ton of active abilities that need to be triggered multiple times per fight, and this grows to an unmanageable amount if you start adding in additional heroes to the army. Focus is taken away from troop positioning and tactics and shifted to micromanaging hero/general abilities. Most formation/unit mechanics have been scrapped in the name of hero power fantasy, which just isn't really my thing.

The campaign map is the more enjoyable part of the game for me. It's quite simple but also enjoyable in that way. I just wish we could go back to recruiting units without needing generals again.

review:
I have over 200 hours in the Total War Warhammer series and it's almost exclusively based on multiplayer experience with friends. While I agree this game series is expensive and the DLC creep is pretty bad, I have to compartmentalize that. I wait for steam sales when everything is cheaper. And sometimes that means you have to wait a long time (can be 1-2 years easily before something is >50% off).

That being said, the game itself is immensely fun and has a ton of depth. I'm a huge fan of the Warhammer universe and it's because of Warhammer that I fell in love with the Total War series. The mechanics, the strategic depth, and the sheer variety of playstyles/gameplay make this something my friends and I can go back to again and again and again.

review:
Magyarul:

Több mint 100 óra után azt tudom mondani, hogy a Total War: Warhammer III alapjátéka ugyan elsőre kevés frakciót tartalmaz, de a rengeteg ingyenes FLC miatt így is nagyon sok mindent ki lehet próbálni. Szerintem ez jó lehetőség arra, hogy az ember eldöntse, akar-e később DLC-ket venni vagy sem.

A nehézség szerintem egész barátságos azoknak is, akik nem nagy stratégák. A Warhammer világában a varázslatok és a hatalmas szörnyek sokszor fontosabbak, mint a tökéletes taktikai tervezés, ezért könnyű belejönni és élvezetes marad casual játékosként is.

A játék egyik legnagyobb hátránya viszont pont ebből jön. Sok érdekes és erős egység csak DLC-ken keresztül érhető el, miközben az AI ugyanúgy használhatja ezeket ellened akkor is, ha neked nincs meg az adott kiegészítő. Ez magasabb nehézségen már érezhető hátrány lehet.

Összességében szerintem nagyon szórakoztató játék, főleg annak, aki szereti a Warhammer világát vagy egy látványosabb, kevésbé hardcore stratégiai élményt keres.

English:

After more than 100 hours, I can say that Total War: Warhammer III may seem to have a limited number of factions in the base game at first, but thanks to the large amount of free FLC content, there is still plenty to try out. It gives players a good chance to decide whether they want to buy DLCs later or not.

I also think the difficulty is fairly friendly even for people who are not hardcore strategy players. In the Warhammer universe, magic and giant monsters often matter more than perfect tactical planning, so the game is easy to get into and stays enjoyable for casual players as well.

The biggest downside comes from this same system, though. Many interesting and powerful units are locked behind DLCs, while the enemy AI can still use those units against you even if you do not own the expansions yourself. On higher difficulties, this can become a noticeable disadvantage.

Overall, I think it is a very fun game, especially for people who enjoy the Warhammer setting or want a more cinematic and less hardcore strategy experience.

English After more than 100 hours, I can say that Total War Warhammer III may seem to have a limited number of factions in the base game at first, but thanks to the large amount of free FLC content, there is still plenty to try out. It gives players a good chance to decide whether they want to buy DLCs later or not. I also think the difficulty is fairly friendly even for people who are not hardcore strategy players. In the Warhammer universe, magic and giant monsters often matter more than perfect tactical planning, so the game is easy to get into and remains enjoyable for casual players as well. The biggest downside comes from this same system, though. Many interesting and powerful units are locked behind DLCs, while the enemy AI can still use those units against you even if you do not own the expansions yourself. On higher difficulties, this can become a noticeable disadvantage. Overall, I think it is a very fun game, especially for people who enjoy the Warhammer setting or want a more cinematic and less hardcore strategy experience.

review:
I’ve really enjoyed playing Total War: WARHAMMER III. The scale of the battles, faction variety, and overall atmosphere are impressive. Each race feels unique, which keeps the gameplay fresh and makes replayability one of the game’s strongest features. The visuals, soundtrack, and world design do an excellent job bringing the Warhammer universe to life, especially during large battles and campaign events.

One thing I particularly liked was the Immortal Empires campaign because it creates a massive sandbox experience with endless strategic possibilities. Diplomacy, army composition, and faction mechanics all add depth to the game. I also appreciated how different legendary lords completely change the playstyle.

On the downside, the game can feel overwhelming for new players due to the amount of mechanics and information presented early on. Performance and AI behavior can also be inconsistent at times, especially in larger campaigns. Some DLC content can feel expensive as well if you want the full experience.

Overall, I would definitely recommend this game to fans of strategy games, fantasy settings, or the Total War series. Despite a few technical and balancing issues, it offers hundreds of hours of content and one of the most ambitious grand strategy experiences available.

Le metí un poco más de una hora y entiendo perfectamente por qué la comunidad vive peleada con este juego. Por un lado, las batallas son espectaculares, el mapa gigante, las facciones tienen estilos bien distintos y cuando una campaña te atrapa, fácilmente se te va toda la noche jugando “un turno más”.

Pero también se siente como un juego que depende demasiado de DLC caros para disfrutarse completo. Hay muchísimo contenido bloqueado y la política de monetización de CA cae mal rápido. Además, la IA a veces hace tonteras, el rendimiento todavía tiene bajones y varios arreglos importantes han terminado viniendo más de mods que del propio estudio.

Aun así, cuando todo funciona, es probablemente de lo más entretenido que hay en estrategia fantástica. Si te gusta Warhammer o los Total War, cuesta soltarlo.  

Tiene momentos increíbles y una escala absurda que pocos juegos de estrategia logran. El problema es que la empresa ha manejado fatal los DLC y el soporte. Se disfruta mucho más con mods y comprándolo en oferta.

review_english: I put a little more than an hour into it and I completely understand why the community is fighting with this game. On the one hand, the battles are spectacular, the map is gigantic, the factions have very different styles and when a campaign catches you, you can easily spend the whole night playing one more turn. But it also feels like a game that relies too much on expensive DLC to be fully enjoyable. There is a lot of blocked content and CA's monetization policy falls badly quickly. Additionally, the AI ​​sometimes does stupid things, performance still has dips, and several important fixes have ended up coming more from mods than from the studio itself. Still, when everything works, it's probably the most entertaining fantasy strategy there is. If you like Warhammer or Total War, it's hard to put it down. It has incredible moments and an absurd scale that few strategy games achieve. The problem is that the company has handled DLC and support terribly. It is much more enjoyable with mods and buying it on sale.

First, I would like to say that this is a good game. It is fun. I will be bashing this game in rest of this review.

Units disappear after the slightest zoom in. There are Reddit posts out there that are 5-6 years old and they still haven't fixed this. I remember encountaring this even in Warhammer 1 by the way, lol! 

Horrible DLC policy. Seriously, look it up. Even worse than Paradox Interactive in my opinion. Paradox at least allows you to play things in multiplayer if the host has the DLCs. 

I remember experiencing bugs in other Total War games that still weren't fixed after 7-8 years when I played Rome 2. This is such a terrible company man... I wish they had a competitor. I mean, they don't have an alternative as far as I know. 

If you don't mind not being able to play 90% of the factions available, being locked out of 50% of your units because you don't have the DLC for them, decade old bugs that keep reappearing, this is a nice game. It really is. 

Oh, forgot the mention. They ban people and remove posts in their own forums when you report bugs and complain. FYI!

review:
Total Warhammer III has its flaws. I mod away things like siege battles, loyalty of generals, diplomatic penalties for having too many allies, obnoxious behavior from vassals, and so on. Even if you like this style of game, it may take some time and effort to find the mods that remove the pain points for your personal preferences.

But that being said, if this is a type of game you like, there is nothing else like it. There's a dozen ways to build your armies that are satisfying and powerful across the different factions, which is great. I've invested a lot of money into buying every single game and all their DLCs, but I've gotten literally thousands of hours of playtime out of it.

I won't say that this game is everyone's 10/10, but for those of us who love it, it is the only place to get our fix.

review:
It's just not fun for me. I love the franchise and I love empire-building games, but this mishmash of two game systems isn't fun for me to play.

Whichever faction I start with, I get overwhelmed within 25 turns. The tutorial is helpful, but then I feel like I'm dumped into a massive sandbox without any further guidance on what I should be doing and what I've been doing wrong.

I know that I lot of people love this game - I wish I was one of them!  I genuinely love Warhammer and I love RTS games. Maybe I'm just bad at the game? If I am, it feels like this is because I was never told what I was supposed to be doing.

review:
This game is good. You'll have fun playing it. You will NOT have fun getting railed by Creative Assembly every time they decide they need a couple more bucks and peddle you a new DLC for an insane price.

The standard is this: you pay 15% of the game's retail price, and Creative Assembly gives you 1 playable lord from a faction that already exists with minimal new units and a new gameplay mechanic that MIGHT work.

The standard USED to be: you pay about 30% of the game's price and you get an entire new faction to play with, or you paid just $1 more than today's prices for TWO playable lords with a multitude of new units and mechanics that WORKED.

This is not the case anymore.

Paradox Interactive has a similar problem in their games, where too much of the content is locked behind DLC paywalls. In Stellaris, they somewhat solved this by offering a $10-a-month subscription that gives you access to all of the game's DLC content for a set amount of time. In an ideal world, DLC is abolished and games are released complete, but with things as they are, I think Total Warhammer would benefit greatly from employing a similar model. But that will never happen.

Creative Assembly doesn't care about you; they care about charging insane prices for mediocre content. I am not confident that they read these reviews.

Consider this before buying.

review: Amazing game, great RTS game with potential for over a thousand hours. At the time of this review, I have around 250 hours on this game, no matter what it says above, and I can conclude that this is one of the greatest games of this age. The real time combat is impeccable, and on par with any RTS game. The campaign map is amazing, and I think sometimes the map is actually harder than the battles. A couple downsides for this game are; the file is really large, but I think they've shrunk it a bit, the game, just like any RTS game, loses its replayability after some time but this one lasts longer than most. Overall, great game, whether you're new to RTS or Total war, or a seasoned player. 8/10.

review:
Its not possible to put a negative recommendation for Total War Warhammer 3 and be honest with myself.

This is a classic game in every sense, when measured by many metrics including how many people were hooked on it, and how much the Warhammer world was influenced by its presence. It did unfortunately fall to sever greed and mismanagement from the owners of its creators Creative Assembly: SEGA.

This is a short review as so much online has been said about it - here are the major pros and cons:

Cons:

1) The game was never completed with all of the necessary factions and elements which would complete the universe such as Southern Realms
2) Naval battles were a big elephant in the room here which would have been perfect in a Warhammer setting. The island battles which replace them have always been immersion breaking for me.
3) The sieges are the worse of any total war game, they also break immersion - and hear me clearly here, why make such an immersive game then break it with such major poor design choices?
4) The slow pace of CA´s improvement of this game despite promises upon promises is legendary to mythical snails, and keeps failing to deliver quality gameplay over more bling.
5) All of the above factors have created one of the gaming industry´s major Catch 22 scenarios - the engine is now outdated, the company lost a lot of good people due to layoffs, and all of the creators of the project fell victim to their own inflated egos - which all led to the promise never delivered likely never to be delivered, as the engine is too old and modern economics limit the interest in doing so. E.g. - the sieges were never fixed as promised, and although there is still talk of that happening its too little too late

Pro:

1) CA did deliver Warhammer Old World to screen, and while I always enjoy the campaign more than the battle, with a couple of mods the game is phenomenal
2) It was still one of the most engrossing and addictive experiences ever leading me to 570 hours and 2000 across the WH series
3) The games brought me into Warhammer fantasy, a great contribution to my life´s entertainment - thank you
4) Despite all the Cons you still occasionally crave for more after many years of its incomplete state.

E.g. - the sieges were never fixed as promised, and although there is still talk of that happening its too little too late Pro 1) CA did deliver Warhammer Old World to screen, and while I always enjoy the campaign more than the battle, with a couple of mods the game is phenomenal 2) It was still one of the most engrossing and addictive experiences ever leading me to 570 hours and 2000 across the WH series 3) The games brought me into Warhammer fantasy, a great contribution to my life s entertainment - thank you 4) Despite all the Cons you still occasionally crave for more after many years of its incomplete state.

review:
Probably the best Total War game (I've only played Shogun and Rome 2 lol) but if you don't have an interest in a specific region/time period then Warhammer 3 is the way to go.

Regrettably, 70% of the roster isn't available in base game and you must buy DLCs for individual lords/pair of lords/sometimes entire factions and its for that reason this game is only a 9/10, but each lord is easily 20-100 hours of gameplay in and of themselves, and there are many lords. If you own Warhammer 1 or 2 or both, your DLCs transfer and you get the lords available in those base games which is pretty cool.

Ultimately I believe this is the apex of Total War and if you have any interest in the genre this would be a good one to buy.

review: You are seen as a cash cow. If you enjoy being milked, go ahead because this will be a perfect match. However, if you on the other hand want a (somewhat) polished strategy game, I suggest you avoid this one. Even if you just come here for the IP you will end up frustrated as every playthrough you will encounter game breaking bugs that will ruin any enjoyment you might find. This game was designed to look pretty and bring in the cash from those who like the IP they are using. So my advice? Safe the money and just watch somebody else play - you will be able to enjoy the pretty visuals and avoid the mess that this game truly is. The only things that would be a pro on the list are superficial, like graphics. Personaly I try to at least get my moneys worth out of it, but this feels like a very abusive relationship. If you are into that and can stomach the points I made above, bend over and open wide.

review:
I've been excited about this series since I first saw the WH1 trailer over a decade ago, and I've been playing it since the day the first game launched. My very first campaign was Kholek Suneater back when the warriors of chaos were awful and people were mad about that very first DLC. I can confidently say that in that ~10 years of my life I've never seen a world built out into a game as well as this trilogy has. It's not without its flaws, there have been many legitimate criticisms over the years and the price of all the content is nothing to scoff at, but the core idea here is the best Total War has ever been (assuming you like fantasy), and fulfills all the dreams I have about this kind of game back when I first played Rome 1.

If you like Warhammer, or fantasy, or strategy games, if anything about this formula piques your interest, then I recommend giving the base game a try. You'll start with limited content but you'll still get to play in the fully formed world with every faction and every unit in Immortal Empires. If you play through that and enjoy it, consider expanding to whatever factions (and eventually lord/unit packs for those factions, you also get content for 1 or more other factions as well) seem coolest to you.

This game is by no means perfect, but it is absolutely excellent despite its flaws and if it's your kind of thing you will not regret picking it up. There will be people who don't love it or it just isn't their thing, others leverage legitimate criticism towards game design or business practices, but all in all I firmly believe it is the most complete realization of a grand strategy world ever put into a video game - most of all it's just really fun.

review:
this game can be fun, but it sucks. to get a complete experiences you have to buy warhammer 1 and 2. plus all the dlc's and then also spend another like 500 euro on warhammer 3 and all its dlc's.

Fights are a major hassel as ai will just micromanage them all no problem while you struggel to care for some cavalry or ranged cavs who will ignore all your commands and get stuck on ♥♥♥♥ no matter how much you click. dont talk about sieges or water combat (which doesn't exist beside games 20 years ago manage to do it), because the hole community think they suck and most people rather auto resolve them then actually play them.

the map with all the dlc's is becoming a major problem where you will have to likely fight 3 legendary lords at turn 5 and i hope you know how to play at that point otherwise its gonne be a new campaign! Dark elves, bretonia, orcs have also not been cared for, for years. Mostly all older factions are much much weaker then there newer counterparts and then i gotta wonder if that is to take more money from you instead of making a fun experiences thats worth your time and money.

It can be fun and yes, warhammer lore is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ great. but, i cant accept the cost and the value when there are so many issue's as well. there are so many mechanics and ♥♥♥♥ that if you are not a pretty skilled RTS player, battles are just gonne feel very frustrating. let alone how much the troops can flee and then rally back, making fights twice as long as they really should be.

IF you decide to wanne play do with extreme care and do not go into dlc's until you REALLY are sure you wanne keep playing. its a lot of ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ money to get the complete experiences and i do regret it.

review:
Ce jeu, c’est la définition de :
« Allez, juste un dernier tour. »

Et ensuite se retrouver à 3h du matin à microgérer l'économie d'un empire qui n’existe même pas avec plus de sérieux que tes propres finances.

Combien il me reste sur mon compte bancaire? Aucune idée.
Combien me rapporte chaque province de mon territoire ? Je peux te le dire a la piece d’or près et t’en faire la projection économique sur 20 tours sans effort.

Mon plan initial était simple : tester différentes factions en bataille personnalisée avant de me lancer dans la campagne principale avec celle qui me correspondait le plus…

J’ai alors découvert que chaque petit bout d’armée, chaque seigneur, et probablement chaque caillou du game possède son propre DLC. Alors je suis repassé à la caisse une seconde fois pour “déverrouiller du contenu”.

Puis je me suis dit : parfait, je vais jouer Archaon, Seigneur de la Fin des Temps. Sauf qu’évidemment…ce con n’est même pas dans la campagne principale. Il est uniquement dispo en bataille et dans le mode : Immortal Empires.

C’est donc après avoir fait chauffer la carte bancaire pour rien, et avec un sérieux niveau de seum, que j’ai fini par cliquer sur Kislev, complètement rincé mentalement.

Heureusement, la faction s'est avérée très fun à jouer… du moins jusqu'à l'apparition des failles du chaos, qui transforment le jeu en ‘’babysitting global’’. 

Pendant que toi tu optimises en construisant les bâtiments anti-failles, tes voisins IA, eux, s’en foutent totalement.
 
Résultat, t'es obligé de poser TES armées chez EUX pour éviter que les démons qui en sortent ne viennent pisser sur TON territoire (parce qu’il vont le faire croyez moi !). Un pur bonheur de management.

C’est également à ce moment que la courbe de plaisir diminue pour une autre raison. Je m’explique:

Le early game est absolument incroyable. Tu joues chaque bataille, la survie des troupes/unités est cruciale et la tension est palpable. 
10/10, mieux que le sexe. (Attention, gros baiseur ici).

C’est au mid-to-late game que tout se casse la gueule. Tu n'as plus du tout besoin de jouer les batailles grâce — ou plutôt à cause  — des buffs en reconstitution complètement fumés. Après, ajoute à ça que tu peux recruter 20 à 40 unités de bonne qualité en un seul tour, alors en perdre n’est absolument plus un problème. A partir de là, ya plus aucune raison de jouer au jeu puisqu’il se joue tout seul. Bof/10

Bref, sur ces 60h de jeu, j’ai passé un temps déraisonnable à autant fixer des écrans de chargement qu'à lutter contre ce launcher de ***** afin de créer une modlist vanilla-friendly et en français.

Si ça peut intéresser quelqu’un : j’ai publié la collection sur le Workshop dans le bon ordre de chargement. 
Le nom de la collection : ANQ0R Vanilla + [FR]

review_english: This game is the definition of Come on, just one last round. And then find yourself at 3 a.m. micromanaging the economy of an empire that doesn't even exist any more seriously than your own finances. How much do I have left in my bank account? No idea. How much does each province in my territory bring me? I can tell you to the nearest gold coin and make the economic projection over 20 turns without effort. My initial plan was to simply test different factions in custom battles before launching into the main campaign with the one that suited me the most. I then discovered that each small part of an army, each lord, and probably each stone in the game has its own DLC. So I checked out a second time to unlock some content. Then I said to myself perfect, I'm going to play Archaon, Lord of the End Times. Except obviously this idiot isn't even in the main campaign. It is only available in battle and in Immortal Empires mode. So it was after heating up the bank card for nothing, and with a serious level of seum, that I ended up clicking on Kislev, completely mentally rinsed. Fortunately, the faction proved to be a lot of fun to play, at least until the Chaos Rifts appeared, which turned the game into global babysitting. While you optimize by building anti-fault buildings, your AI neighbors don't care at all. Result, you are obliged to station YOUR armies in THEM to prevent the demons that come out from coming to piss on YOUR territory (because they will do it, believe me!). Pure management joy. This is also when the pleasure curve decreases for another reason. Let me explain. The early game is absolutely incredible. You play each battle, the survival of the unit troops is crucial and the tension is palpable. 10 10, better than sex. (Be careful, big fucker here). It’s in the mid-to-late game that everything breaks down. You no longer need to play battles at all thanks to, or rather because of, completely smoked reconstitution buffs. Afterwards, add to that that you can recruit 20 to 40 good quality units in a single turn, so losing them is absolutely no longer a problem. From there, there is no reason to play the game since it plays itself. Bof 10 In short, over these 60 hours of play, I spent an unreasonable amount of time staring at loading screens as much as fighting against this launcher in order to create a vanilla-friendly modlist in French. If anyone is interested, I published the collection on the Workshop in the correct loading order. The name of the ANQ0R Vanilla FR collection

review:
Ultra units size sucks and I hate every single person who plays on ultra unit size. I also hate CA for thinking "Alright there are lots of people playing on ultra so we should balance the game around ultra even though it is a garbage unit size that completely ♥♥♥♥♥ everything up." Large is fine, large units also have lots of people but it doesn't result in the insane blobbing you see on ultra, it doesn't make flanking take an eternity, it doesn't make the AI worse, it doesn't mess up settllement battles, it doesn't turn pulling your units back and maneuvering into a new form of torture. It is also better for people who have older hardware.

"Me like big battle me watch more men fight" ♥♥♥♥ you. ♥♥♥♥ you. Please go watch a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ movie, I want to play a strategy game.

This is not the only reason for my negative review of course. Warhammer 3 is actually quite a fun game. HOWEVER, it has all the typical problems of a modern total war game. It is slop. Very fun slop, don't get me wrong. But still slop. 

Am I going to continue playing it? Yes. But that doesn't change the fact that CA needs to go bankrupt as soon as possible.

review:
It is not obvious why to buy III over II, or vice versa.
But every following part of the game, the DLCs policy becomes even more crazy.
To max out your experience for the TW:WH1 you need 90 additional euro, TW:WH2 - 120 euro.

Without discounts, the base game costs 60 euro, and all the DLCs 421 euro. It is a joke.
There would be not Complete Edition, may be, in 10 years, and each following DLC would cost you as the base game during a holiday season (every big sale).
I get it, developing games become very expensive business, but selling DLCs for a price of 1/2...3/4 of the base is a rip-off.

Would I recommend the game? Yes
Will you be (somehow) limited without DLCs? Sadly, but yes.
Would I recommend the whole package? No

review:
I love this series. I've sunk hours and hours into it. The factions are so different, starting a new campaign as a new race promises a genuinely fresh and unique experience. The world and units are wonderfully realized-- the game really draws you in.

You know what's REALLY cool? There are TWO delete save game buttons. They look exactly the same. One deletes the saved games you have selected, and the other deletes your whole campaign! Isn't that cool?

Whoever made the decision to include that feature, GFY. Thumbs down on that basis alone.

1. trying to hide my units and set up an ambush, the enemy will always know exactly where your forces are at all times.

review: If you enjoy being stuck in the same place on the map, unable to see most of the entire time due to unbalanced campaign difficulty this is the game for you. On normal all enemies will hunt you down on the map even if there are enemies close by them and their enemies will allow them to cross their land so they can do it. Even on easy you are just playing border patrol and playing wakamole on all your cities behind it. Even if you have a good economy you cant have enough armies to do anything else except defend once you confederate the north and the east. You are constantly fighting for the same territory and playing defense the vast majority of the time. I wanted to see other kinds of enemies but get use to only seeing about 3 different types because you cant move. Its a great fantasy border patrol game though, not really a good conquer the map game like total war is usually trying to be. Dont waste as much time as I did.

review: By far one of the most time wasting games ive played and i am all in for it. Yes all the dlc's are just greed straight up but if you build it up or uhh what not, the game gives you just endless hours of gameplay. Each faction, even lords in factions all play different and all have some type of learning curve that will have you going "hmmm, ok how tf do i play these guys". I will say, it is a game that takes a minute to really click but once it does be prepared to wonder where your day went. I will say, for me, ive always learned best cheating ( first 80 hours i cheated even lol) and understanding how things work, so thats my best advice for going into it. Also learn you won't win every battle and if you even do, just learn where it went wrong and go from there. I'd say it's right up there next to total war shogun 2 for me. 5/5 game!

review:
Definitely one of the greatest strategy game ever created. I love whole franchise and Warhammer world in general. If you play it right without any exploits it is very balanced and it gives you a unique set up every time. Yes its expensive but earn the money and buy it, be a man. So far I finished twenty two out of twenty four campaigns on legendary difficulty. And some campaigns of the fractions and lords that I really like I played like tens or even hundreds of times on all possible difficulties. Every fraction has its own advantages and perks, you just have to find it and use it wise. Yes AI is not perfect but I like how it challenges you and hits you in the back with the first opportunity. If you really want to feel the real struggle and challenge I recommend to play it only on legendary difficulty. Other difficulties are too easy. You don't have to conquer the whole world. Complete the campaign steps and defeat your main enemies. If you get bored you can always add some mods. 
PS Looking forward for Total War Warhammer 40k.

review:
The game was broken on launch, great after fixing and now is in such an unblanaced state that it just isnt nearly as fun anymore. 
Every other DLC releases with insanely overpowered units and/or lords that just never gets fixed, the AI is either braindead or actively gangs up on the player and some Factions are so behind on updates that their campaigns are considered generally unplayable.
I would genuinely recommend Warhammer II over 3 as it is just smaller in general, so there is less powercreep and faction overload to be had and you're really only missing out on kislev, Kathay and the chaos dwarves. 
It is still a nice looking game, just incredibly shallow at times and just not fun to play at others.

review:
Etant un énorme fan de warhammer depuis l'enfance, que ce soit le jeu de plateau ou simplement l'univers et tout ce qui peut en découler, j'ai été heureux de tomber sur le total warhammer 1 a l'époque sans respecter le jeu de plateau et en manquant de réel aspect stratégie, ou gestion, mélangeant juste absolument tout dans un gloubiboulga plutot bordelique mais plaisant à jouer. 

Pour en revanche ne pas me sentir respecter que ce soit en tant que gamer, ou simplement client voir la même formule quasiment vierge de modifications ou d'amélioration des campagnes ainsi que de l'aspect stratégie à part sur la prise de ville, le total Warhammer 2 et 3 et ses campagnes (ainsi que leurs DLCS innombrable) n'apporte rien à part un ajout massif de contenu Payant tuant absolument tout le multijoueur en ligne à moins de passer à la caisse (20 a 30 euros par race pour tout leur héros et unités, 300/350 pour le jeu complet en plus de l'achat du jeu).


Petite note pour les nouveaux sur Total Warhammer sachez que ce que l'on appelle une campagne dans ce jeu n'a rien à voir avec une campagne du type Warcraft, Dawn of War, etc .., non une campagne ici c'est juste une ou deux missions spécifique et 2/3 lignes de dialogues glissé sur le cotés et ensuite demerde toi sur la carte du jeu avec ses 40 factions non jouables avec le produit de base. Sans aucun objectif ou histoire précise.

Donc en gros si vous êtes fan de Warhammer et de jeux de stratégies vous pouvez passer un bon moment à tenter l'une des quelques "campagnes" gratuites, mais si vous êtes juste un gamer vous vous sentirez juste escroqué d'avoir accés à 1/20ème des campagnes ainsi que de leur contenue même hors campagne (soit leur héros, unités etc ...) après avoir payer 60 balles (jeu n'ayant pas diminué depuis sa sortie il y à 4 ans).

Donc pour résumé en ce qui me concerne, malgré une bonne idée de mélange des licenses et un bon début, l'entreprise gérant tout ça n'est maintenant qu'un vendeur de rêve omnubilé par rentabilisé à tout prix ce produit dans toutes ses déclinaisons sans fournir le moindre effort supplémentaires en matière d'optimisation du jeu en lui même, des campagnes ou de tout autres parties de ce jeu ... 
J'ai été déçu juste en ouvrant le menu solo et en voyant quasiment tout le contenu grisé et payant, j'ai malgré tout lancé l'une des seules campagnes gratuites parmis l'une des seules races gratuite (WouhOuu) pour voir, et après quelques heures monotone et identique aux volets précédents des années après j'ai souhaiter être remboursé mais Hé ! vous avez ouvert le jeu, c'est mort aucun remboursement ! :D
Bref en ce qui me concerne énorme deception, je ne regarderais même plus leur prochaines sorties, pas de temps à perdre avec des arnaqueurs féneant.

review_english: Being a huge fan of Warhammer since childhood, whether it be the board game or simply the universe and everything that can result from it, I was happy to come across Total Warhammer 1 at the time without respecting the board game and lacking any real strategy aspect, or management, just mixing absolutely everything in a rather messy but pleasant to play gloubiboulga. On the other hand, to not feel respected whether as a gamer, or simply a customer seeing the same almost blank formula of modifications or improvement of the campaigns as well as the strategy aspect apart from the capture of a city, the total Warhammer 2 and 3 and its campaigns (as well as their countless DLCS) brings nothing apart from a massive addition of Paid content absolutely killing all online multiplayer unless you go to the checkout (20 to 30 euros per race for all their heroes and units, 300 350 for the full game in addition to purchasing the game). Small note for those new to Total Warhammer, know that what we call a campaign in this game has nothing to do with a campaign like Warcraft, Dawn of War, etc., no, a campaign here is just one or two specific missions and 2 or 3 lines of dialogue slipped on the sides and then screw yourself on the game map with its 40 factions not playable with the base product. Without any specific objective or story. So basically if you are a fan of Warhammer and strategy games you can have a good time trying one of the few free "campaigns", but if you are just a gamer you will just feel cheated by having access to 1 20th of the campaigns as well as their content even outside the campaign (i.e. their heroes, units etc...) after paying 60 balls (game having not decreased since its release 4 years ago). So to summarize as far as I'm concerned, despite a good idea of mixing licenses and a good start, the company managing all of this is now just a dream seller obsessed with making this product profitable at all costs in all its variations without providing the slightest additional effort in terms of optimizing the game itself, the campaigns or any other parts of this game... I was disappointed just by opening the single player menu and seeing almost all the content grayed out and paid for, I nevertheless launched one of the only free campaigns among one of the only free races (WouhOuu) to see, and after a few monotonous hours and identical to the previous parts years later I wanted to be reimbursed but Hey! you opened the game, it's dead no refund! D In short, as far as I'm concerned, a huge disappointment, I won't even watch their next releases, no time to waste with lazy scammers.

review:
The jungle trembles. The drums roar. And then she arrives.

TW:W III has unleashed pure feline fury with the new Bhashiva DLC, and it might be one of the boldest additions the game has seen in years. Forget dusty politics and slow empire-building — Bhashiva storms onto the battlefield like a myth ripped straight out of a forbidden Cathayan legend.

The Tiger Warriors are savage, fast, and absolutely glorious to watch in combat. Every charge feels cinematic, every clash sounds brutal, and the faction drips with personality. From the striking tiger-themed aesthetics to the aggressive campaign mechanics, this DLC doesn’t just add content — it injects fresh blood into Warhammer III’s already chaotic world.

What makes Bhashiva truly shine is how different the campaign feels. Instead of grinding through another familiar conquest route, you’re constantly pushed toward relentless momentum, hunting enemies with predatory precision while stacking powerful relics and battlefield bonuses. It’s a campaign built for players who want speed, violence, and style.

Creative Assembly also deserves credit for taking a deep-cut piece of Warhammer lore and transforming it into something fans genuinely didn’t expect. The tiger warriors were once little more than obscure whispers hidden in old sourcebooks — now they’re center stage, tearing through armies like they own the Mortal Realms.

Sure, there are a few rough edges. Some players have pointed out balancing quirks and lingering technical issues, but when the battles begin, it’s hard not to get swept away by the sheer spectacle.

Bhashiva isn’t just another DLC lord.

She’s a statement.

A feral, roaring reminder of why Total War: Warhammer III still dominates the fantasy strategy genre after all these years.

Много подобного бреда .. ФУ одни нервы от боевки от тактики и стратегии нечего зависит, особенно воюя с компьютером , который управляет одномоментно 20 отрядами, а у тебя бараны стоят и тупят смотрят как одни тикают а других убивают ..... У компа конница делает стремительную атаку а уходит на второй заход, твою конницу свяжут боем даже обычная пехота, мортиры которые потратив 1 4 боезапаса не может уничтожить обычный отряд стрелков людоящеров это что за дичь !? ..... короче ужас подобного много ! ... Жаль нельзя уже вернуть деньги за игру и DLC ....
review_english: Implementation of the battle pa asha! For some reason, the squad stops finishing off the enemy squad and stands watching it tick, your squad doesn’t start attacking the nearest squad, it just stands and watches as your other squad is destroyed nearby, you need directions to manually attack, the enemy squad that wavered (especially bad when they are horsemen), he can return when inspired and stab in the back and destroy the warriors from whom he previously wavered, and if they chased him further, they would go off the map and that’s it..... A lot of similar nonsense .. FU, some nerves from the battle, nothing depends on tactics and strategy, especially when fighting with a computer that controls 20 units at a time, and you have sheep standing there dumb and watching as some tick and kill others..... The computer's cavalry makes a swift attack and leaves for the second run, your cavalry will be tied up in battle even by ordinary infantry, mortars which, having spent 1 4 ammunition, cannot destroy an ordinary squad of riflemen What kind of game are lizardmen? ..... in short, there is a lot of horror like this! ... It's a pity that you can't get your money back for the game and DLC....

review: Was actually having quite a bit of fun until i got stuck on a campaign map at the same spot over and over again, looked up on the internet to find out yes the Enemy AI does indeed cheat... instead of just make the AI better it literally pulls armies out of thin air and economy means nothing to them, 15 settlements or 1, they will still have full 20 unit armies fresh and ready to go 3 turns after a huge battle... just really feels bad man. Gonna try a No Cheat Mod and see if it makes me want to change my review.
